speaker: interviewee
text: What happened in their place, my daughter?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Can we finish that story? She was punished for the sugar, and then what happened afterwards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Even if I could find her, but I don't know. She was-- that woman with [INAUDIBLE], she was in [RUSSIAN]. And I don't think she would be run somewhere else, because she had no one else. But because she treated so badly my daughter as a child, if I could find her, I wouldn't. The German woman who-- excuse me, it's entirely different story. But the other one, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were beginning to say something about a second anecdote about your young daughter when she was alone with that woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: You were starting your second story about your daughter when she was very young with that woman. She was hungry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. That's right. The building was a big square. And all around there were little flats, and old, very old, I don't know, like a square court. There is a entrance. You go in, like a stony wall. In the old times, very old times, there were those courts. And inside were doors to go in from one flat to another, one near each other. Those people, I don't know whether Poles or whether Ukrainians, but they must have known about or suspected something, because they knew there was two children. Suddenly, she has three, one more. So maybe they suspected. But we were always afraid that neighbors, non-Jewish neighbors, will give us out. They never did. They probably had pity on that child, a little child and no mother, no father. I mean, they have a heart, too, that people. And when it-- my daughter told me later, she was older, few years old. She remembers. She remembers from three. She says even earlier, she remembers. told me he remembers from two. I remember from three but not from two. He said he remembers from two, possible, two and a half. But I think she was three then. And she said it was Christmas time. I could see, I could hear singing carol songs. And I was alone. And [? Georgia-- ?] the Polish woman-- her children were somewhere in the market or somewhere else. She went and gave them food. But no one gave me nothing. But those people-- as I went, I didn't want to be all the time alone inside. So I went out, outside. And a woman opens a door. And it was evening for Christmas. And she threw out a potato. Just, she didn't give me. She didn't call me. But she threw out. And it fell outside. And I picked it up and ate it. And it was a baked potato. And it-- to me, it was like the best chocolate, she said. And that was for Christmas that another woman gave her. But [INAUDIBLE] is the one who kept it. She didn't give her anything. And yet, when after the war they want to take away from her, she screamed. And she threw stones after them. It means she wanted her only to give her to, for Jesus marry of Jesus Christ to offer her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you come to have another sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the one survivor-- it was here already, before when the first time to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was in '64. I read in the Jewish news a story. I read a story in the Jewish news that a mother, a sole survivor, a Holocaust survivor lost her husband and two, two grown-up sons to Hitler. She was alone, had nowhere to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: She went to Israel. Where should she go, an old, old woman? She was already old, because she got old in the war. And when the war started, she wasn't young anymore. I can see [NON-ENGLISH] Street in Tel Aviv, because I lived eight years in Tel Aviv.

speaker: interviewee
text: She went to [NON-ENGLISH]. It was in the paper next, written. And suddenly, she thought, I can't believe it. It's not true. I don't dream. I walk here. I don't dream. Opposite her, she saw her eldest son, who for years, she believed that he's dead. And the son saw her and came on. And she said, no. I am dreaming. He said, no, no, Mother. It's me, what name his was. I survived. And I didn't know about you. And I went to Israel. She went years later. He lived already there, and well, maybe established. And so happily, she found her son. And I, and I thought, being here, I thought, my daughter was already married. And I thought if it happened to her, why can't anything happen to me-- had brothers and sisters. And I, I don't know how my brother-- he ran to Russia, but I don't know how he died. And maybe he survived somewhere. So if it happened to her, it can happen to me. But I'm not going straight to Israel. Who knows about the best of people who survived is the HIAS in New York. And I thought, I'll go to New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Scraped of the money what I had there and left my daughter here a nice family, sisters-in-law, mother-in-law-- it's OK. I go to New York. I will go to-- anything what happened, I could do it. I just want to do it. How will you do? Yes, I'll go by ship. I'll go. And he said, but you go to something uncertain. It's not certain that because it happened to her, it will happen to you. And why to New York, not to, to Israel? I said no, because the HIAS knew about. Here in the Welfare Society-- it was then Welfare Society-- I gave them the names them whom I lost, who I, it's possible that they alive. And searched, but they couldn't find anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went, went to New York. There are some distant, not even my relatives, my late husband's rela-- distant ones. And in Atlanta, a second cousin. I have to have someone to go there. But I remembered there was my husband's, not mine, his cousin. Oh, he must be old now, older than him and older, much older than me. Myer was his name. So they went to United States before the war. And I had one address, the one in Atlanta. I thought if I have an address, I'm not going to a hotel straight away. I have no idea where to go. I've never been to New York. I'll go to her. I have an address. All right. I went by boat, by ship, went not knowing anything and not believing that I will find anything. I went and arrived in Atlanta, went to that distant cousin to ask if I can stay a few days with her, that I came. I want to go to New York, to Atlanta

speaker: interviewer
text: She said do you know anyone in New York? She had a sister there in New York, too. I met them later. She gave me an address to go for a day. But everybody expected me. They thought of people surviving Hitler will come to United States in rags, old, looking, you know, shaggy hair. Here came an elegant young woman, dressed better than them and they are dress-- I always liked to dress-- and looking much better they. They in shacks in here. They in New York for years, and they live in, like in, in Europe in the old shtetls. She had a look at me, said, how do you want to go to New York? I said, by Greyhounds. How do you Grey-- they thought here in Australia, they are on top. They are cultured people. We here, we know nothing about. How do you know about Greyhounds? I said, that's funny. How do you know? They have Greyhounds in Israel, too. It's a bus, you know, Greyhounds. By bus-- I didn't need to go by trains. It's not very far from Atlanta. It's distant, but all right. She said, I'll ring up Myer. That was only one of my late husband's-- he was alive. I don't know now if he's alive, a cousin. His cousin, not mine.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember him faintly. I was a little girl, maybe starting school. And he was already a grown-up man when they left. I've heard at home, you know, people left for the golden medina, for America, everybody talked. You're going to America, a rich country. And probably, they will get wealthy there, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everybody knew that Myer went there. He was much older than my late husband. That woman Atlanta rang straight away to that Myer to meet me on the stop where the Greyhounds. You know, it's exactly where the Greyhounds and train-- how do you call it? You know, where the trains are stopping.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. He had to meet me there. And he knew when the Greyhound buses stops there. So he had to meet me. But he, again, expected a [NON-ENGLISH], something that he would ashamed to go in the street with. And he was a tall man. He got-- since I was maybe six or seven when they left, I knew about it, remember. Because they lived not far from Grodek.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he had to come and meet me-- I came with a case-- and go to that, she died in Israel later, of my late husband's cousin. She and her, and her husband, they lived there. And what was the street? I remember the street, East side-- not far from Bronx somewhere. It was quite distant. So I had to walk there. And how he looked, they couldn't tell me-- a man with a, with a dark hat and a dark coat, a tall man. That was that. When I arrived there in New York already, I had in pocket on the address where I have to go. But it was night. And it was very far. I had no idea where it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to look up the street and ask people. And I was afraid to go by underground train. And there were stories always, if you, if you are alone in a, a train, the, the blacks will murder you. It's not true. They're only stories. They helped me. I tell you later. And so you wait for him there, she said. I rang him. He knows. And he will meet you there. All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: People came out from this train. People came out from people waiting, hugging, kissing. I was standing alone, waiting for Myer. Where's Myer? Looked here, looked there, no Myer. He didn't come. What will you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. The first thing, I put the, to store the case, not of even a heavy case. I store it. You know, there are a-- for a few pennies, you put in it. It opens outside the, the walls, the station, pushed it in, closed it. I had the key, safe. I'm alone now, free. If I have to walk, I can pick it up tomorrow or tell that, that distant cousin where it is they can pick it up for me and waited for Myer. Now, I was left alone. No one is there. People left with, you know, people waiting for them, picking them up. Everybody left. I was alone at night time. I'm in New York, the first time alone, looking here. There was no Myer. Then I walk this way. And man, a middle-aged man, even older than middle-aged, walked that way. I walk here. He walks there. He doesn't look at me, and I don't look at him. There is a law. To a strange woman, if you look, if you talk to her, it's prison for this in the United States. You can't speak to a stranger on the street. So he didn't look at me. He went there, straight down. And I went here. Suddenly, I thought, maybe it's Myer. He expect me. I knew it. Survivor, Hitler survivor, from the camps, he expect maybe an old woman in [NON-ENGLISH]. And here, he was afraid to look at me even. I thought, I'll do something about it. Maybe it's him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he looked-- what he thought I would look like, he looked like that in New York. And as he passed by, I said to myself, Myer? Just to myself-- Myer? He said, yes, yes. I said, that's Myer. Why didn't you come before? Why didn't you ask me before? I was afraid, such an elegant person. I, I thought that will come after the war someone in the rags here. And that-- that's you? They didn't-- they thought that people here, they sent [NON-ENGLISH] here from United States to give the people, to the welfare here. And I, whenever I worked, I dressed myself as-- I didn't care much about food, but I wanted to be dressed. So we went, and out our case. And we went back over to that cousin. And he left. He left. And I slept there. She couldn't believe it, such a long way to come to look for, for relatives there, if sisters and brothers. I said, who else could help me? I had to do it. I told her the story about in Israel, that woman, and then said that inspired me. And that's why I came. And they couldn't believe it, what I did. They live there like in ghettos in United States, people. And he said, a doctor, medicine, and a lawyer, they started in Poland, both and the lawyer. But he wasn't recognized United States. You know how they had to study again? So he worked with the That was a newspaper, a Jewish newspaper, there where he worked with his two degrees, that he was elderly already, more than middle-aged, that cousin of my husband. They went later to Israel and both died in Israel. So I stayed there. That other, that Myer left. And next day, I said, why did you come here so for? They live like in ghettos there. They eat like in ghettos and live like in Ameri-- people think that America. It's much better in Australia than the United States, much better here, cleaner, a better life here. Maybe there are others, those people that went from Jewish small places. They live just as they lived before in, in Europe. So next day, what will you do? I will go to the HIAS? How will you find it? They live in New York, in a big city. And I came from Australia. How will you find it? I said, what do you mean, how I find? Look up the telephone book. Yes, you'll find it in telephone book? And how will you go so far? I said, don't worry. I will not, I'll not take a tram, a train or a bus. I will walk. What? You'll walk? You know, It's other side of New York. I will walk. Why? Because if I go by bus or train, I'll miss it. If I walk, I look up the street. And I will find it. I haven't forgotten-- 166 Lafayette Street. I remember. I don't know. Everything is in my, in my head. Lafayette Street, that is the HIAS. As you can find it up now-- Lafayette, New York, 166.

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I-- it's in my mind, you know, so deeply embedded, because I had to find it. And I didn't know where. And no one can, could help me. So I walked and walked, I walked out early in the morning, eight o'clock. And I came in the afternoon about 4:00. And I walked. And I came there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I came to the building, came in. And it's written HIAS here there. And I went up there. I don't remember. Was it second or third floor? I didn't want to go by a lift, so I walked up. I walked once. Now I can't walk, but then I walked, kilometers. And I came in the HIAS. I remember a glass door. I opened. Behind a desk sat a man. And I said, I came from Australia, because I, I look for my relatives, for survivors after the war. He said, what's your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, when he found out that I live, lived in Lwow, he said, I'm from Lwow, too. Before the war, he went. And he worked, worked in the HIAS, an older man. And when I told him my name, maiden name, but it was my brother's name, he said, it rings a bell.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I thought maybe he knows about my brother. He said, no. It's your sister is alive. The first time after so many years, it was in '64, that I found out that my sister lives in France.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, why he knew about my sister, because a friend, her friend that they were together during the German occupation, somewhere there in Germany working. She lived in Israel after the war. She found her mother alive and her sister. And she lived in Israel. In Israel. From my brother-in-law-- I don't know if he's alive now-- from him somehow, she found out that I am alive, But she didn't know it was in Australia. It was in Germany still when he found out. So she knew that I'm alive and that my daughter's alive in Germany. She visited in France, my sister, and told her that I, I survived. My sister didn't know about it. But she knew. She found out in Israel from my brother-in-law, I wrote them, that I am alive and my daughter but not my husband. My sister is Ella, a Jewish name. She was [? Kristina ?] during the war, [? Kristina Pavlik. ?] I think I wrote it there, married to a Frenchman, non-Jewish man, who helped her, too. And she got married to him. So she knew. And she wrote or rang-- I don't know-- to HIAS that she want to find out where I am now, because that was years ago when I was in Germany that her sister-- it's me and my daughter-- that we survived. But she doesn't know where we are now. But she gave her address in France. When I came to the HIAS, I didn't know about it. She knew that I am alive. But I didn't know that she's alive. When I came to the HIAS, they said the name Spiegler, because she gave her maiden name as Spiegler. It rings a bell. And I know it-- for a moment, I, I thought, I thought of, of a miracle, maybe my brother. I didn't believe that she's alive, but I thought my brother. Because I haven't heard from her. And my brother, I knew that he ran to Russia. And I thought maybe somewhere he survived. They opened a drawer and got out-- yes, [? Kristina Pavlik, ?] Ella Spiegler, my sister. Again, after so many years, he thought maybe I will faint to overcome me, nothing. I stood like a stone. So straight away, they gave a cable for me. And we talked from there, from the office there. And I even recognized her voice. And straight away, I booked again a boat, went to Germany. And her husband, non-Jewish, when I arrived, knife he said, look, Wanda. You can stay with us. At first, I didn't go straight to their place. We went to-- the nearest where the boat docked was where the film festivals are.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cannes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cannes, yes. It was in Cannes where we met for the first time. We looked at each other. We recognized each other, but many years. We were young when both left but still recognized each other. He didn't want to come near me, her husband. I don't know if he was a anti-Semite or not. I don't know what it was with him. He felt something. He rescued her, in a way, like [INAUDIBLE] rescued me. That was with them, because he was in a camp where only French were. And she was where Poles were, opposite. But they visited each other. It wasn't so like where I was, that we couldn't congregate with other people. And somewhere, he fell in love wot her. She was very beautiful. And then same thing-- because she was in the office working there and got a little piece of bread in Germany where she worked as a Christian, a piece of bread, maybe more. Instead of payments, they gave them a piece of bread. And people were jealous, others, you know, those peasants. And they said, oh, she must be Jewish. Because the same was with me, very similar, because she has a better head, she can work in the office. Not much what was the work in office, to write down names maybe. Though he came up and defended her, said you said it once more, she's my girl, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which camp was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Which camp was she in?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in-- I forgot it. Not far from where I was in Germany, Meiningen, not far.

speaker: interviewer
text: And looking back now, your time in Australia--

speaker: interviewee
text: And somehow she survived, and he brought her to France. And for 20 years, he was afraid to say-- I don't know if he knew that she's actually Jewish or not. My sister said that he, she doesn't know. Open, they never talked. And when she cried at nighttime in bed, she cried often, because again, that she was younger than I am. She was the youngest. And later was little brother of 13, that he was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, if you don't stop crying, I don't know what I will do with you. And she didn't want to say why she is crying. She just said, because I don't know anything about my family. She didn't want to say this.

speaker: interviewee
text: 20 Years after the war already went, his parents lived. And they all liked her very much. She couldn't tell him, because he would never accept if she is Jewish. And maybe he felt something, I don't, she doesn't know. But he never, he never would say anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when I arrived there, and in so many years after the war, and I said, why are you afraid? We haven't got any more afraid? Why are you afraid to say, I'm Jewish? So he said, don't tell it. If you want to stay as not Jewish here, you can stay in my place as long as you wish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I said him, if I have to hide my name and hide myself as a, as a non-Jewess, I better leave. And I won't stay here with you. And I think he got a heart attack, because he was, you know, worrying all the time, because I said it open. And the people knew later. I said it open.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, why do I have to hide, again here, in France? And said, you hate Jews? He said no, but my wife is not Jewish. I said, you know. You knew that she's Jewish. I think he didn't go to work because he was ashamed. Thank you. We'll continue next tape.
